content_en:
The commercial law section of Rawdat al-Talibin covers the full range of contractual transactions in exhaustive detail. This section of the Rawdah is particularly valued by later Shafi'i scholars because it clarifies the authoritative school position on numerous contested questions where earlier authorities had disagreed.

The chapter on sales (bay') opens with the conditions for a valid contract: the legal competence of both parties, the unambiguous expression of offer and acceptance, the known and existent object of sale, and its deliverability. The Shafi'i school's position on khiyar al-majlis — the option that allows either party to cancel the sale while they remain in the same meeting — is affirmed on the basis of the hadith 'the two parties to a sale have the option of cancellation as long as they have not separated' (Bukhari and Muslim). This position is shared with the Hanbali school but rejected by the Hanafis.

Forbidden sales are catalogued with their Shafi'i-specific reasoning: the sale of a runaway slave, the sale of what one does not yet own, two sales in one contract (bay'atayn fi bay'ah), and the urban dweller selling on behalf of a Bedouin stranger who does not know the market price. The Shafi'i rules on bay' al-salam (advance purchase where goods are not yet in existence) and bay' al-mu'awadah (exchange sales) are presented with the conditions necessary to avoid gharar (harmful uncertainty).

The sections on rental (ijarah), partnership (shirkah), loans (qard), and collateral (rahn) follow the same systematic approach. Al-Nawawi's treatment of mudarabah (profit-sharing investment) clarifies the rights and obligations of the capital provider and the working partner, the conditions for valid profit-sharing ratios, and the liability of the mudarib in cases of negligence. The book's commercial law section remains a primary reference for Shafi'i muftis and Islamic finance practitioners operating within the Shafi'i framework.
